title: Configuration Settings Compliance — CIS Benchmark Control Checks

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIST 800-53 CM-6 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 CM-6 (Configuration settings) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.14.56: Configuration Settings Compliance — CIS Benchmark Control Checks.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.14.56' running on tenable:sc compliance exports or custom cis:assessment sourcetype, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060), SCAP/OVAL via HEC
spl:
index=compliance sourcetype=cis:assessment earliest=-7d control_result="FAIL" benchmark="CIS_Microsoft_Windows_Server_2022"
| stats count by hostname, control_id, severity
| sort - severity, - count
description: Ingests configuration assessment scan results for CM-6 configuration settings.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST 800-53 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes compliance and sourcetypes cis:assessment. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Normalize control_id to CIS references; (2) join system owner lookup; (3) SLA remediations; (4) map to SC-7 for boundary controls when network-related. Indexes required: `index=compliance`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=cis:assessment`.
visualization: Table (failed controls), Bar chart (by benchmark), Single value (fail count). Key fields: `control_id`, `hostname`, `severity`.
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for ingests configuration assessment scan results for configuration settings. This matters because it catches problems that could lead to audit findings or compliance gaps.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060), SCAP/OVAL via HEC.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `tenable:sc` compliance exports or custom `cis:assessment` sourcetype.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Normalize control_id to CIS references; (2) join system owner lookup; (3) SLA remediations; (4) map to SC-7 for boundary controls when network-related.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=compliance sourcetype=cis:assessment earliest=-7d control_result="FAIL" benchmark="CIS_Microsoft_Windows_Server_2022"
| stats count by hostname, control_id, severity
| sort - severity, - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Configuration Settings Compliance — CIS Benchmark Control Checks (CM-6)** — Ingests configuration assessment scan results for CM-6 configuration settings.

Documented **Data sources**: `tenable:sc` compliance exports or custom `cis:assessment` sourcetype. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060), SCAP/OVAL via HEC. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: compliance; **sourcetype**: cis:assessment. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=compliance, sourcetype=cis:assessment, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by hostname, control_id, severity** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (failed controls), Bar chart (by benchmark), Single value (fail count)
